text: The surgeon uses a robotic arm equipped with monopolar scissors or shears to complete the dissection of the pulmonary fissure.

The surgeon visually inspects the middle lobe, with the lower lobe positioned at the bottom of the field and the upper lobe visible in the distance. The upper lobe shows minor adhesions to the chest wall, which are not addressed. The middle lobe appears small, allowing the lower lobe to expand into the space without requiring removal of adhesions.

The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery located below the middle lobe, revealing a well-defined fissure. The dissection progresses to complete the fissure, providing clear control and visualization of the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery with the Intuitive XI robot to perform precise dissection, avoiding the charcoal buildup associated with bipolar Maryland cautery.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the anterior oblique fissure, revealing a thin fissure between the middle and lower lobes. The middle lobe vein is visible anteriorly, followed by the middle lobe bronchus, and one or two segmental pulmonary artery vessels are located posteriorly.

The surgical field is viewed through the Intuitive Robot's camera, positioned centrally and low, providing an 8x magnified 3D view. The phrenic nerve is visible to the right of the shears. The surgeon identifies the middle lobe vein and lifts a lymph node at station R-eleven I, located between the middle and lower lobes, for meticulous en bloc removal.
The surgeon uses the Cadia instrument to gently remove a lymph node in its entirety.
The surgeon carefully removes a lymph node en bloc using robotic instruments, ensuring a complete R0 resection by avoiding piecemeal extraction.
The surgeon carefully removes the R-eleven lymph node located between the middle and lower lobes using robotic instruments, ensuring it is taken out on block to achieve an R-zero resection.

The surgeon provides a clear view of the basal pulmonary artery and the middle lobe bronchus, visible just below the shears. Above the shears, the middle lobe is seen. The surgeon carefully avoids penetrating the lymph node, maintaining clear tissue planes and anatomical boundaries around the pulmonary artery and bronchus.

The surgeon's assistant introduces a peanut instrument to remove the R-Eleven lymph node through an anterior lower utility incision. The camera is positioned in the eighth rib space alongside the cadillac and scissors. A fourth port is used for the third arm, often equipped with another cadier for lobe retraction if needed. Blunt dissection is employed to identify anatomical structures and manage any bleeding by applying pressure with peanuts or swabs.

The surgeon uses CO2 insufflation to create space for robotic arms, enhancing the view of the middle lobe pulmonary vein. Blunt dissection with a peanut and monopolar shears is performed to identify the upper border between the middle and upper lobe pulmonary veins. The dissection proceeds anteriorly, providing a clear view without needing to flip the middle lobe posteriorly.
Tristan adjusts the robotic arms, placing the Maryland forceps in port arm two and the Cadiere forceps in port arm four, facilitating blunt dissection around the pulmonary vein.

The surgeon uses Cadillac forceps and Maryland instruments to perform blunt dissection around a vessel, demonstrating the precision of robotic surgery with enhanced visual feedback through eight times magnification and 3D vision. The anatomical landmarks are clearly visible, allowing careful manipulation of the vessel without tactile feedback.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the middle lobe bronchus while observing the pulmonary vein to ensure it does not bulge or bow. Using the Maryland forceps, the surgeon rotates the instrument and separates tissue around the vein, ensuring the tips of the instrument are visible before proceeding. This technique allows for precise dissection around veins and arteries using robotic assistance.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the vessels, ensuring not to poke them, and isolates the vein by looping around it. The vein is lifted, and a stapler is introduced using the second arm, which has been swapped out for this purpose. The procedure is performed using a three-arm technique, suitable for this simple case.
The surgeon introduces the intuitive stapler, a white thirty, through the posterior port to staple the bronchus. The bronchus is virtually exposed, and the pulmonary vein is clearly visible.

The surgeon performs a final inspection around the bronchus, which is clearly visible due to optimal camera positioning. The border next to the pulmonary artery is exposed, revealing the middle lobe pulmonary arteries, typically consisting of medial and lateral branches (A4 and A5). The Maryland forceps are positioned above the arteries, with the bronchus visible below.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using Cadillac forceps anteriorly and Maryland forceps posteriorly, carefully separating tissue while monitoring the anatomy to avoid hitching. The superior pulmonary vein to the upper lobe is visible anteriorly, and the surgeon ensures no accidental pressure is applied to the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a blunt cadia instrument to navigate around the back of the middle lobe bronchus, observing tissue movement and avoiding unnecessary force. The robotic approach provides a down-up view from the bottom of the chest, allowing visibility of both sides of the vessel. A zero-degree camera is used for this procedure.

The surgeon addresses minor bleeding from the middle lobe by using electrocautery to buzz the area, ensuring hemostasis. The robotic instruments are carefully maneuvered without poking, maintaining precision in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a blunt Cadier to safely maneuver around the pulmonary artery, ensuring no damage is done. The middle lobe bronchus is slung in preparation for stapling. The surgeon demonstrates the potential firing of the stapler from the posterior port, with the option to perform additional dissection around the artery before proceeding.

The surgeon uses a peanut dissector to locate the pulmonary arteries, focusing on the A-four artery, the lateral segmental artery to the middle lobe. The upper lobe shows a few adhesions, which are not addressed due to the small size of the middle lobe. The horizontal fissure is clearly visible, aiding in the identification of the artery.

The surgeon uses a robotic approach to safely maneuver around a pulmonary artery, visible in the distance, ensuring no tissue is hitching up. A vessel loop is placed around the artery for control, with careful camera adjustments to zoom in and out as needed for optimal visualization.
The surgeon removes the Maryland dissector and inserts a white 30 load stapler using the Xi robotic system, demonstrating the ease and control of stapling during the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses the instrument exchange mechanism of the SI system to position the white 30 stapler on the lateral middle lobe segmental artery. The stapler is fired, and the screen displays the blade advancing and retracting. After firing, the grip is opened, revealing a clear view of the A-V medial segmental artery and the pulmonary artery with a well-defined fissure. The area is confirmed to be safe for further dissection, with no significant structures behind the artery except a possible anterior segmental artery to the upper lobe.

The surgeon passes another vessel loop around the pulmonary artery, preparing for stapling. A white thirty vascular load is introduced and advanced with minimal articulation, allowing for a straight shot. The arteries and veins have been addressed, leaving only the bronchus and an easily accessible fissure to complete.
The surgeon fires the stapler on the medullobronchus, completing the final part of the pulmonary lobectomy procedure. A small drain is placed, with minimal air leak and blood loss observed.
The surgeon performs the final firing on the horizontal fissure using a robotic stapler, completing the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
